definition: (Skt anything round, disk, circle, globe, orb, ring, circumference; district, territory, province, country; multitude, collection, whole body) 1. circle, orb, district, territory, surface. See also āditya-, agra-, aṇḍa-, bhūmi-, deśadig-, dig-, gṛha-, hīra-, jagat-, Kurukṣetra-, pari-, prabhāwa-, prajā-, pṛthiwī-, rājya-, raṇa-, rawi-, sabhā-, sakala-, samara-, swarga-, tejo-, trelokya-, tribhuwana-, wāda-, wiwara-, Yawa-, Yawadwīpa-. 2. sacred (magic) circle, within which ceremonies are performed (esp. tantric?). See also Śiwa-. 3. abode of a religious community. See also śewa-. 4. head of a maṇḍala (religious community) 5. the path or orbit of a celestial body

definition: (Skt maṅgala, happiness, felicity, welfare, bliss; anything auspicious or tending to a lucky issue, e.g. a good omen, prayer, benediction, auspicious ornament or amulet, a festival or any solemn ceremony on important occasions; maṅgalya, auspicious, lucky; an auspicious prayer, any auspicious thing; in OJ maṅgala and maṅgalya, q.v., are used without difference in meaning). Maṅgala indicates ``anything that brings blessing, ensures success or a happy issue'', hence a ceremony or ritual, a prayer or mantra, the introductory parts of parwas and kakawins, addressed to a god or a sage. The person who possesses the salutary power of blessing, purifying or assuring success (god, king, holy man etc) as well as the sacred text, purifying or sanctifying those who hear or read it, are maṅgala too. Hence it is used for him who is most prominent, the leader among the many, going in front, foremost in battle etc (the meaning coming close to that of mukhya, wĕkas). Finally, maṅgala often indicates the musical instruments which go in front of a royal procession (announcing the coming of the king as maṅgala?) or of an army. See also ādi-, agra-, āśraya-, aśubha-, dewa-, dur-, jaya-, pādyādi-, rāja-, raṇa-, śānti-, sarwa-, stuti-, śubha-, wiwāha-.

38.22: laksmi niṅ puri: maṅunĕṅ galuh (among the ``sarwa rumambat''). This raises all kinds of botanical problems of importance for the understanding of comparisons and descriptions: the hair seems to be compared to this plant, the flowers are worn in the hair, and it serves as a shampoo to make the hair shiny and luxuriant. What is the connection with gaḍuṅ? Are they twined together?

etc. The following passages present difficulties. Could marma mean ``going as far as'', ``moreover''?

4.138: aḍuh susuhunan sampun de niṅ kalepyan riṅ aṅalanâpet sandi, dadi kaṭuṅka de niṅ mar#mâneṅ ati (``cares''?). Some notable examples of marma with a negative: AW 1.7: rwekāṅ ādi Tilottamā ... lawan Suprabhā / tapwan marma tuhun lĕhĕṅ-lĕhĕṅa saṅke rūpa saṅ hyaṅ Ratih. definition: 1. (Skt) illusion, unreality; deception, fraud; sorcery, magic, unreal or illusory image. See also pāśa-, strī-, yoga-. 2. reflection, image, likeness, resemblance, that which is seen vaguely or in a glimpse, trace. See also puṣpa-. 3. showing vaguely what is behind, thin (of fabric, ḍapur maya, limar maya, etc) transparent, pure, luminous, glimmering, gleaming, shining (esp. softly and seen at a distance? cf maya-maya, amaya-maya). See also ati-.
